ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: The Author's Defence of Origen.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But since carping detractors have imposed upon many persons and have succeeded in deterring them from reading Origen, as though he were a blasphemous writer, I deem it not unseasonable to make a few observations respecting him.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Worthless characters, and such as are destitute of ability to attain eminence themselves, often seek to get into notice by decrying those who excel them.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: And first Methodius, bishop of a city in Lycia named Olympus, labored under this malady; next Eustathius, who for a short time presided over the church at Antioch; after him Apollinaris; and lastly Theophilus.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This quaternion of revilers has traduced Origen, but not on the same grounds, one having found one cause of accusation against him, and another another; and thus each has demonstrated that what he has taken no objection to, he has fully accepted.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For since one has attacked one opinion in particular, and another has found fault with another, it is evident that each has admitted as true what he has not assailed, giving a tacit approbation to what he has not attacked.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Methodius indeed, when he had in various places railed against Origen, afterwards as if retracting all he had previously said, expresses his admiration of the man, in a dialogue which he entitled Xenon.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But I affirm that from the censure of these men, greater commendation accrues to Origen.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For those who have sought out whatever they deemed worthy of reprobation in him, and yet have never charged him with holding unsound views respecting the holy Trinity, are in this way most distinctly shown to bear witness to his orthodox piety: and by not reproaching him on this point, they commend him by their own testimony.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But Athanasius the defender of the doctrine of consubstantiality, in his Discourses against the Arians continually cites this author as a witness of his own faith, interweaving his words with his own, and saying, ‘The most admirable and assiduous Origen,’ says he, ‘by his own testimony confirms our doctrine concerning the Son of God, affirming him to be co-eternal with the Father.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Those therefore who load Origen with opprobrium, overlook the fact that their maledictions fall at the same time on Athanasius, the eulogist of Origen.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: So much will be enough for the vindication of Origen; we shall now return to the course of our history.
